title: El alfabeto y la pronunciación / The alphabet and pronunciation
bridge: Your native alphabet has 26 letters. The target alphabet has 27, including the letter 'ñ'. Many letters sound similar, but there are important differences: vowels are pure and never change; 'h' is always silent; 'j' sounds like the English 'h' in 'hot'; 'll' sounds like the English 'y'; 'ñ' sounds like 'ny' in 'canyon'; 'c' before 'e' or 'i' sounds like 's' (or 'th' in Spain); 'g' before 'e' or 'i' sounds like the Spanish 'j'; 'v' and 'b' are pronounced the same (a soft 'b'). The most frequent mistake for English speakers is pronouncing the Spanish 'j' as the English 'j' (as in 'jump').
rule: The target alphabet has 27 letters: a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h, i, j, k, l, m, n, ñ, o, p, q, r, s, t, u, v, w, x, y, z. Key pronunciation rules: Vowels (a, e, i, o, u) are short and pure. 'C' is hard (like 'k') before a, o, u or consonants; soft (like 's' or 'th') before e, i. 'G' is hard (like 'g') before a, o, u; soft (like an English 'h') before e, i. 'H' is always silent. 'J' is always like the English 'h' (never like the 'j' in 'jump'). 'LL' is like 'y' in 'yes' (or 'sh' in some regions). 'Ñ' is like 'ny' in 'canyon'. 'R' is trilled at the beginning of a word or after 'l', 'n', 's'; otherwise a single tap. 'RR' is a strong trill. 'V' and 'B' are identical: a soft bilabial stop (not labiodental).

common_error.explanation: You are using the English 'j' sound for the Spanish letter 'j'. In the target language, 'j' is always like the English 'h' in 'hot' (a soft, breathy sound). For example, 'jamón' should sound like 'hah-MOHN', not 'jah-MOHN'. This is the most common pronunciation error for English speakers.

examples.note: Always short and pure, never like the 'a' in 'cat' or 'day'.
In English, 'b' and 'v' are different. Here, they are the same. 'Boca' and 'vaca' start with the same sound.

title: Saludar, presentarse y despedirse / Greetings, introductions and farewells
bridge: In English you have 'Hello', 'Good morning', 'What's your name?', 'My name is...', 'Nice to meet you', and 'Goodbye'. In the target language, greetings change depending on the time of day: 'Buenos días' (until lunch), 'Buenas tardes' (until evening), 'Buenas noches' (evening/night). To introduce yourself, you say 'Me llamo...' (I call myself...) or 'Soy...' (I am...). To say 'Nice to meet you', you say 'Mucho gusto'. For farewells: 'Adiós', 'Hasta luego', 'Hasta mañana'. Your most common mistake is using 'Buenas noches' as 'good night' only when going to bed, but it's also used as a greeting in the evening. Also, English speakers often forget to use the inverted exclamation mark (¡) at the beginning.
rule: Greetings: ¡Hola! (Hello); ¡Buenos días! (Good morning); ¡Buenas tardes! (Good afternoon); ¡Buenas noches! (Good evening / Good night). Introductions: ¿Cómo te llamas? (What's your name?) – Me llamo... / Soy... (My name is... / I am...). ¿Cómo estás? (How are you?) – Bien, gracias (Fine, thanks). ¡Mucho gusto! (Nice to meet you!). Farewells: ¡Adiós! (Goodbye); ¡Hasta luego! (See you later); ¡Hasta mañana! (See you tomorrow); ¡Chao! (Bye – informal). Remember: Use ¿ at the beginning of questions and ¡ at the beginning of exclamations.

common_error.wrong: ¿Cómo te llamas? – Yo soy llamo Ana.
common_error.explanation: You used 'Yo soy llamo' which is a direct translation of 'I am called' with a non-existent structure. The correct way to say your name is 'Me llamo Ana' (I call myself Ana) or 'Soy Ana' (I am Ana).

examples.note: Used as greeting in the evening, or as farewell at bedtime.
Used when you expect to see the person again soon.

title: Saludos, despedidas y cortesía / Greetings, farewells and politeness
bridge: In English, we say 'hello', 'goodbye', 'thank you', 'please', 'excuse me', and 'I'm sorry'. In Spanish, it's similar: 'hola', 'adiós', 'gracias', 'por favor', 'perdón', 'lo siento'. The main differences are: 1) Spanish distinguishes between formal and informal greetings ('buenos días' vs 'hola'), and 2) 'Perdón' is used for minor apologies or to get attention, while 'lo siento' is for deeper regret. English speakers often use 'I'm sorry' for everything, so they need to learn when to use 'perdón' vs 'lo siento'.
rule: Use 'hola' for informal greetings, and 'buenos días/tardes/noches' for formal or time-specific greetings. For farewells, use 'adiós' (final/formal), 'hasta luego' (see you later), 'hasta mañana' (see you tomorrow). For politeness: 'gracias' (thank you), 'por favor' (please), 'perdón' (excuse me / I'm sorry for minor things), 'lo siento' (I'm sorry for serious things). Respond to 'gracias' with 'de nada'.

common_error.explanation: Using 'lo siento' (deep regret) for a minor apology or to get attention is incorrect. In Spanish, use 'perdón' or 'disculpa' for such situations. 'Lo siento' is reserved for expressing sympathy or regret for something serious.

examples.note: If you'll meet the next day.

examples.translation: Thank you very much for your help!
Excuse me, I didn't understand. Can you repeat?
examples.note: Using 'perdón' to get attention or apologize.

examples.original: Lo siento mucho, ha sido culpa mía.
examples.translation: I'm very sorry, it was my fault.
examples.note: Using 'lo siento' for a serious apology.

examples.translation: Hello, good morning, how are you? (formal)

examples.original: Hasta luego, que tengas buen día.
examples.translation: See you later, have a good day.
examples.note: Farewell with a wish using subjunctive 'que tengas'.

title: Artículos determinados e indeterminados / Definite and indefinite articles
bridge: In your language, you have the definite article 'the' and indefinite articles 'a' and 'an'. In the target language, definite articles (the) change according to gender and number: 'el' (masculine singular), 'la' (feminine singular), 'los' (masculine plural), 'las' (feminine plural). Indefinite articles (a/an) also change: 'un' (masculine singular), 'una' (feminine singular), 'unos' (masculine plural, meaning 'some'), 'unas' (feminine plural, meaning 'some'). The biggest mistake English speakers make is using the article with professions: in English you say 'I am a teacher', but in the target language you say 'Soy profesor' (no article). Also, you tend to say 'the life is beautiful' (general concept), while the target language omits the article? Wait, correct error: Actually Spanish uses the definite article for general concepts ('La vida es bella'), so the error is the opposite: English speakers often OMIT the article in Spanish where it is required. For example, 'Life is beautiful' → 'Vida es hermosa' (incorrect), should be 'La vida es hermosa'.
rule: Definite articles: el (masculine singular), la (feminine singular), los (masculine plural), las (feminine plural). Indefinite articles: un (masculine singular), una (feminine singular), unos (masculine plural = 'some'), unas (feminine plural = 'some'). Use definite articles with general concepts and abstract nouns (La vida es bella). Use indefinite articles with countable nouns the first time you mention them (Tengo un perro). Do NOT use indefinite articles with professions, nationalities, religion, or unmodified nouns after 'ser' (Soy profesor, not 'Soy un profesor'). Also, do NOT use indefinite articles after 'tener' when talking about age (Tengo 25 años, not 'Tengo unos 25 años' unless you mean 'about').

common_error.explanation: In English, you say 'I am a teacher' with the indefinite article 'a'. In the target language, when stating your profession, nationality, or religion after the verb 'ser', you do NOT use the indefinite article. 'Soy profesor' is the correct form. This is the most frequent article mistake for English speakers.

examples.note: Use definite article for general concepts (contrary to English).

title: Dar y pedir información personal / Giving and asking for personal information
bridge: In English, you ask for personal information with questions like 'What is your name?', 'How old are you?', 'Where are you from?', 'What is your phone number?', 'What is your address?'. In the target language, the questions are similar but with different structure. For age, you use 'tener' (to have): '¿Cuántos años tienes?' (How many years do you have?) – you answer 'Tengo 25 años' (I have 25 years). For nationality, you use 'ser': '¿De dónde eres?' (Where are you from?) – 'Soy de Inglaterra' or 'Soy inglés'. For profession, you use 'ser' without article: 'Soy profesor' (I am a teacher). The most common mistake for English speakers is using 'ser' for age (e.g., 'Soy 25 años') and forgetting that age is expressed with 'tener'. Another mistake is using the indefinite article with professions ('Soy un profesor') or using 'estoy' for nationality.
rule: Key questions and answers: Name: ¿Cómo te llamas? – Me llamo... / Soy... Age: ¿Cuántos años tienes? – Tengo [number] años. Origin/Nationality: ¿De dónde eres? – Soy de [country]. / Soy [nationality]. Profession: ¿A qué te dedicas? / ¿Cuál es tu profesión? – Soy [profession] (no article). Phone number: ¿Cuál es tu número de teléfono? – Es el [number]. Address: ¿Dónde vives? – Vivo en [address]. Email: ¿Cuál es tu correo electrónico? – Es [email]. Remember: For age, always use 'tener', never 'ser'. For profession, never use 'un/una' after 'ser'.

common_error.explanation: You used 'ser' (to be) to express age, calquing the English 'I am 25 years old'. In the target language, age is expressed with 'tener' (to have): 'Tengo 25 años' (I have 25 years). This is one of the most common and persistent errors for English speakers.

examples.translation: What's your name? – My name is Ana.
How old are you? – I am 30 years old (I have 30 years).
Where are you from? – I am from Mexico.
Are you Spanish? – Yes, I am Spanish.
What do you do? – I am a student.

examples.original: Mi número de teléfono es el 555-1234.
¿Cuál es tu dirección? – Vivo en la calle Mayor, número 5.
examples.translation: What is your address? – I live on Mayor street, number 5.

translations.en: giving and asking for personal information

title: Números cardinales y ordinales / Cardinal and ordinal numbers
bridge: In English, numbers are similar: 'one, two, three, four, five...' In Spanish, they are 'uno, dos, tres, cuatro, cinco...'. The main differences are: 1) Spanish uses 'uno' before a masculine noun becomes 'un' (e.g., 'un libro'), and 'una' before feminine nouns (e.g., 'una casa'). 2) Numbers 16-19 are written as one word (dieciséis, diecisiete, etc.), unlike English where they are separate (sixteen, seventeen). 3) Ordinal numbers in Spanish agree in gender and number with the noun (primero/primera, primeros/primeras), while in English they are invariable. 4) The apocope of 'primero' and 'tercero' before a masculine singular noun: 'primer' and 'tercer' (e.g., 'el primer día').
rule: Cardinal numbers: 0 cero, 1 uno, 2 dos, 3 tres, 4 cuatro, 5 cinco, 6 seis, 7 siete, 8 ocho, 9 nueve, 10 diez, 11 once, 12 doce, 13 trece, 14 catorce, 15 quince, 16 dieciséis, 17 diecisiete, 18 dieciocho, 19 diecinueve, 20 veinte, 30 treinta, 40 cuarenta, 50 cincuenta, 60 sesenta, 70 setenta, 80 ochenta, 90 noventa, 100 cien (or ciento), 101 ciento uno, 200 doscientos, 1000 mil. Ordinal numbers: 1º primero (primer before masculine singular noun), 2º segundo, 3º tercero (tercer before masculine singular noun), 4º cuarto, 5º quinto, 6º sexto, 7º séptimo, 8º octavo, 9º noveno, 10º décimo.

common_error.explanation: The error is writing 'cinquenta' with a 'q' (influence from French 'cinquante' or English 'quarter'). In Spanish, it's always 'cincuenta' with a 'c'. Also, English speakers may say 'diez y seis' instead of 'dieciséis'.

examples.translation: sixty, seventy, eighty, ninety, one hundred
two hundred, three hundred, four hundred, five hundred
I live on the fifth floor.

examples.note: Apocope of 'primero' to 'primer' before masculine singular noun.

title: Género y número de sustantivos y adjetivos / Gender and number of nouns and adjectives
bridge: In your language, nouns do not have gender, and adjectives do not change form. In the target language, every noun has a gender (masculine or feminine), and adjectives must agree with the noun in both gender and number. This is a completely new concept for you. Most nouns ending in -o are masculine (el libro), and most ending in -a are feminine (la casa). But there are many exceptions (el día, la mano, el problema). Adjectives usually end in -o for masculine and -a for feminine (bonito/bonita). For plural, add -s or -es. Your most common mistake is using the masculine form of the adjective with a feminine noun (e.g., 'la casa bonito'), or forgetting to make the adjective plural. Also, you tend to use 'el' with feminine nouns that end in -a but are actually masculine (e.g., 'el problema' is correct, but you might say 'la problema').
rule: Gender: Most words ending in -o are masculine (el gato). Most ending in -a are feminine (la casa). Exceptions: el día (masculine), la mano (feminine), words ending in -ma, -pa, -ta from Greek are masculine (el problema, el mapa, el planeta). Number: To form the plural, add -s if the word ends in a vowel (libro → libros), add -es if it ends in a consonant (papel → papeles). Adjectives: they agree in gender and number with the noun they modify. For masculine singular: -o (alto), feminine singular: -a (alta), masculine plural: -os (altos), feminine plural: -as (altas). Adjectives ending in -e or consonant do not change for gender (grande, azul), but do change for number (grandes, azules).

common_error.explanation: You used the masculine form of the adjective 'bonito' with the feminine noun 'casa'. In the target language, adjectives must match the gender of the noun. Since 'casa' is feminine, you need the feminine form 'bonita'. This is a very common error because English does not have grammatical gender.

examples.note: Plural: add -s to both noun and adjective.

examples.original: el coche azul / la casa azul
examples.translation: the blue car / the blue house
examples.note: Adjective 'azul' does not change for gender, only for number (azules).

translations.en: gender and number of nouns and adjectives

title: Describir personas, objetos y lugares / Describing people, objects and places
bridge: In English, you describe things using adjectives like 'big', 'small', 'red', 'tall', 'beautiful'. In the target language, you also use adjectives, but they must agree in gender and number with the noun. For people, you use 'ser' for physical descriptions (height, personality) and 'estar' for temporary states (health, mood). For objects, you use 'ser' for permanent qualities (material, color) and 'estar' for location or temporary condition (dirty, broken). For places, you use 'ser' for inherent characteristics (beautiful, big) and 'estar' for location. Your most common mistake is using the wrong form of the adjective (masculine instead of feminine, singular instead of plural) or placing the adjective before the noun (like in English). Also, you often confuse 'ser' and 'estar' when describing people (e.g., 'Es aburrido' vs 'Está aburrido').
rule: Describing people: Use 'ser' for physical traits (Soy alto), personality (Soy simpático), and profession (Soy profesor). Use 'estar' for mood (Estoy contento), health (Estoy enfermo), and appearance when it's temporary (Hoy estás guapo). Describing objects: Use 'ser' for material, color, size (La mesa es de madera, La casa es grande). Use 'estar' for location (El libro está en la mesa) or temporary state (La sopa está fría). Describing places: Use 'ser' for inherent qualities (Madrid es bonito). Use 'estar' for location (El museo está en el centro). Adjective agreement: adjectives change to match gender (alto/alta) and number (altos/altas). Word order: adjectives usually go AFTER the noun, unlike English (a red car → un coche rojo).

common_error.explanation: You placed the adjective 'roja' before the noun, following English word order. In the target language, descriptive adjectives typically come after the noun. The correct order is 'una casa roja' (a red house). This is a very common error for English speakers.

examples.translation: The table is made of wood.

examples.original: La playa está cerca del hotel.
examples.translation: The beach is near the hotel.

title: Colores, formas y tamaños / Colors, shapes and sizes
bridge: In English, colors are invariable (they don't change with gender or number). In Spanish, colors agree in gender and number with the noun they modify, except for a few that are invariable (e.g., 'azul', 'verde', 'gris', 'marrón', 'rosa'). For example: 'camisa roja' (red shirt, feminine), 'coche rojo' (red car, masculine). English speakers often forget to change the color adjective to match the noun's gender, saying 'camisa rojo' instead of 'roja'. Also, the position of adjectives: in English they go before the noun ('red car'), while in Spanish they usually go after ('coche rojo'). The exception is when an adjective is used for emphasis or with certain short adjectives like 'gran' (apocope of 'grande' before a singular noun).
rule: Colors: rojo/roja, azul (invariable), verde (invariable), amarillo/amarilla, negro/negra, blanco/blanca, gris (invariable), marrón (invariable), rosa (invariable), naranja (or anaranjado/anaranjada). Shapes: círculo (m), cuadrado (m), triángulo (m), rectángulo (m). Sizes: grande (apocope 'gran' before masculine singular nouns), pequeño/pequeña, mediano/mediana, largo/larga, corto/corta. The adjective usually goes after the noun.

common_error.explanation: The error is not matching the color to the noun's gender. 'Camisa' is feminine, so the adjective must be feminine: 'roja'. English speakers are used to colors being invariable, so they forget to change the ending.

examples.original: La casa es grande y blanca.
examples.translation: The house is big and white.
examples.note: Size + color (agreement in gender and number).

examples.original: Mi coche es más pequeño que el tuyo.
examples.translation: My car is smaller than yours.

examples.original: Prefiero el color rosa al naranja.
La mesa es rectangular y marrón.
examples.translation: The table is rectangular and brown.

examples.note: Apocope of 'grande' to 'gran' before masculine singular noun.

title: Presente de indicativo (verbos regulares) / Present indicative (regular verbs)
bridge: In your language, the present tense is simple: you say 'I speak', 'you eat', 'he lives'. In the target language, the present tense also exists, but verbs are conjugated differently depending on their ending: -ar, -er, -ir. You remove the ending and add specific endings for each subject pronoun. The good news: the patterns are regular and predictable. The most common mistake for English speakers is forgetting to change the verb ending for each subject (e.g., using the same form for all persons), or applying English rules like adding '-s' only for he/she/it. In the target language, every subject has its own ending.
rule: For regular verbs: -ar verbs: hablo, hablas, habla, hablamos, habláis, hablan. -er verbs: como, comes, come, comemos, coméis, comen. -ir verbs: vivo, vives, vive, vivimos, vivís, viven. Use the present tense for: actions happening now, habitual actions, general truths, and future actions when time is specified (Mañana voy al cine). Note: The subject pronoun is often omitted because the verb ending indicates the subject, but you can include it for emphasis or clarity.

common_error.wrong: Yo hablo, tú hablo, él hablo.
common_error.explanation: You used the same verb form 'hablo' for all subjects. In the target language, each subject has its own ending. 'Tú' requires 'hablas', and 'él' requires 'habla'. English speakers often apply the English pattern where only the third person singular changes ('I speak', 'you speak', 'he speaks'). This is incorrect here.
common_error.ejemplo_1: Yo hablo, tú hablas, él habla.

title: Hablar de gustos e intereses / Talking about likes and interests
bridge: In English, you say 'I like pizza', 'I love music', 'I don't like sports'. In the target language, you use the verb 'gustar' (to please) with indirect object pronouns: 'Me gusta la pizza' (pizza pleases me). For stronger likes, you use 'encantar' (to love): 'Me encanta la música'. For dislikes, you use 'no me gusta' or 'odiar' (to hate). Your most common mistake is using 'gustar' like a normal verb: 'Yo gusto la pizza' (incorrect). Another mistake is forgetting the plural form: 'Me gustan los libros' (not 'gusta'). Also, you may confuse 'gustar' with 'querer' (to want) when expressing likes.
rule: Use 'gustar' for likes: me gusta (I like), te gusta (you like), le gusta (he/she/you formal like), nos gusta (we like), os gusta (you all like), les gusta (they/you all like). For plural items, use 'gustan': me gustan los libros. Use 'encantar' for love: me encanta (I love). For dislikes: no me gusta / no me encanta. To ask about preferences: ¿Qué te gusta hacer? (What do you like to do?). Answer: Me gusta + infinitive (Me gusta nadar) or Me gusta(n) + noun (Me gusta la pizza). Other verbs: 'preferir' (to prefer) – Prefiero el té (I prefer tea).

common_error.explanation: You used 'gusto' as a regular verb, following the English structure 'I like coffee'. In the target language, 'gustar' works backwards: the thing liked is the subject, and the person is the indirect object. The correct form is 'Me gusta el café' (coffee pleases me).
common_error.ejemplo_2: A mí me gusta el café.

examples.original: ¿Qué te gusta más, el cine o el teatro?
examples.translation: What do you like more, movies or theater?

title: Días, meses y estaciones / Days, months and seasons
bridge: In English, days of the week and months are capitalized (Monday, January), but in Spanish they are not (lunes, enero). English speakers often capitalize them out of habit. The days of the week are similar but different: 'Monday' is 'lunes', 'Tuesday' is 'martes', etc. Months are also similar: 'January' is 'enero', 'February' is 'febrero', etc. Seasons: 'spring' is 'primavera', 'summer' is 'verano', 'autumn' is 'otoño', 'winter' is 'invierno'. The main difficulty is the accent on 'miércoles' (Wednesday) and 'sábado' (Saturday), and the spelling of 'septiembre' (September) which English speakers might spell with a 't' (like 'September') but in Spanish it's 'septiembre' or 'setiembre' without the 't'. Also, the preposition used with days: in English we say 'on Monday', but in Spanish it's 'el lunes' (with the article) and no preposition. For months, we use 'en' + month (e.g., 'en enero').
rule: Days: lunes, martes, miércoles (with accent), jueves, viernes, sábado (with accent), domingo. All are masculine. Months: enero, febrero, marzo, abril, mayo, junio, julio, agosto, septiembre, octubre, noviembre, diciembre. Seasons: primavera, verano, otoño (with accent), invierno. Use 'el' before days (e.g., 'el lunes') for 'on Monday'. Use 'en' for months and seasons (e.g., 'en enero', 'en verano').

common_error.explanation: Two errors: 1) Capitalizing the day (in Spanish, days are not capitalized). 2) Missing the accent on 'miércoles'. English speakers often forget to use accents and incorrectly capitalize days and months because they are used to doing so in English.

examples.translation: On Monday I have a meeting.

examples.original: Mi cumpleaños es el 15 de agosto.

title: Verbos irregulares frecuentes en presente / Common irregular verbs in present tense
bridge: In your language, irregular verbs like 'to be', 'to have', 'to go' change form unpredictably (I am, you are, he is). In the target language, there are also common irregular verbs that you must memorize. The most important ones are 'ser' (to be – permanent), 'estar' (to be – temporary), 'tener' (to have), 'ir' (to go), 'hacer' (to do/make), 'poder' (can/to be able), 'querer' (to want), 'saber' (to know), 'dar' (to give), 'decir' (to say), 'venir' (to come), 'ver' (to see), 'oír' (to hear). Your typical mistake is applying regular endings to these verbs (e.g., 'Yo sabo' instead of 'Yo sé'), or confusing 'ser' and 'estar' (both 'to be' but used differently). Also, you might forget stem changes like in 'poder' (puedo, puedes, puede...).
rule: Common irregular verbs in present: ser: soy, eres, es, somos, sois, son. estar: estoy, estás, está, estamos, estáis, están. tener: tengo, tienes, tiene, tenemos, tenéis, tienen. ir: voy, vas, va, vamos, vais, van. hacer: hago, haces, hace, hacemos, hacéis, hacen. poder: puedo, puedes, puede, podemos, podéis, pueden. querer: quiero, quieres, quiere, queremos, queréis, quieren. saber: sé, sabes, sabe, sabemos, sabéis, saben. dar: doy, das, da, damos, dais, dan. decir: digo, dices, dice, decimos, decís, dicen. venir: vengo, vienes, viene, venimos, venís, vienen. ver: veo, ves, ve, vemos, veis, ven. oír: oigo, oyes, oye, oímos, oís, oyen. You need to memorize these forms. Pay special attention to stem-changing verbs (e→ie, o→ue, e→i).

common_error.explanation: You applied the regular -er verb ending to 'saber' (which would be 'sabo'), but 'saber' is irregular. The correct first-person form is 'sé'. This is a very common mistake for English speakers because they expect regular patterns. Memorize the irregular forms.

examples.note: Ir: voy, vas, va, vamos, vais, van.

title: Expresar obligación y necesidad / Expressing obligation and necessity
bridge: In English, you express obligation with 'I have to...', 'I need to...', 'I must...'. In the target language, you use 'tener que + infinitive' (to have to), 'necesitar + infinitive' (to need to), and 'hay que + infinitive' (one must, impersonal). For example: 'Tengo que estudiar' (I have to study), 'Necesito dormir' (I need to sleep), 'Hay que llegar temprano' (One must arrive early / You have to arrive early). Your most common mistake is forgetting to conjugate 'tener' correctly (e.g., 'Yo tener que trabajar') or using the personal 'hay que' with a subject (*'Hay que yo estudie'). Also, you might confuse 'necesitar' with 'necesitar de' (the preposition is not needed).
rule: Tener que + infinitive: conjugate 'tener' (tengo, tienes, tiene, tenemos, tenéis, tienen) + que + infinitive. Example: 'Tengo que estudiar' (I have to study). Necesitar + infinitive: conjugate 'necesitar' (necesito, necesitas, necesita, necesitamos, necesitáis, necesitan) + infinitive. Example: 'Necesito comer' (I need to eat). Hay que + infinitive: impersonal, only one form. Example: 'Hay que trabajar' (One must work). To express necessity of an object, use 'necesitar' + noun: 'Necesito un libro' (I need a book). Negation: 'No tengo que salir' (I don't have to leave), 'No necesito nada' (I don't need anything).

common_error.explanation: You forgot to conjugate 'tener'. In English, 'have to' only changes to 'has to' for third person. In the target language, 'tener' must be fully conjugated: 'tengo', 'tienes', 'tiene', etc. The correct first-person form is 'tengo que'.

examples.translation: One must study to learn / You have to study to learn.
You have to arrive on time.

title: La familia / The family
bridge: In English, we say 'father, mother, brother, sister, grandfather, grandmother, uncle, aunt, cousin'. In Spanish, it's very similar: 'padre, madre, hermano, hermana, abuelo, abuela, tío, tía, primo, prima'. The main differences are: 1) Spanish has a distinction between 'padres' (parents) and 'parientes' (relatives). English speakers often use 'parents' for both, but in Spanish 'padres' only means 'father and mother'. 2) Spanish has grammatical gender for all family members, including 'primo' (male cousin) vs 'prima' (female cousin), while English uses 'cousin' for both. 3) The possessive adjectives in Spanish agree with the noun (mi, tu, su, nuestro), similar to English, but English speakers sometimes forget to use the article with possessive adjectives (e.g., 'el mi casa' is incorrect, just 'mi casa').
rule: Family members: padre (father), madre (mother), hijo/hija (son/daughter), hermano/hermana (brother/sister), abuelo/abuela (grandfather/grandmother), tío/tía (uncle/aunt), primo/prima (cousin), sobrino/sobrina (nephew/niece), cuñado/cuñada (brother-in-law/sister-in-law), suegro/suegra (father-in-law/mother-in-law). To say 'parents' (mother and father), use 'padres' (plural masculine). To say 'relatives' (extended family), use 'parientes'. Possessive adjectives: mi (my), tu (your, informal), su (his/her/your formal/their), nuestro (our), vuestro (your plural, Spain). They go before the noun and agree in number (but not gender for the possessive itself: mi/mis, tu/tus, su/sus).

common_error.explanation: The error is using 'parentes' (relatives) to mean 'parents' (father and mother). In Spanish, 'padres' means 'parents' (the couple), while 'parientes' means 'relatives' (extended family). English speakers often confuse 'parents' with 'relatives' because in English 'parents' is only the immediate couple, but they may think 'parentes' sounds similar to 'parents' and use it incorrectly.
common_error.ejemplo_2: Mis parientes viven en Francia. (if you mean extended family)

examples.original: Mi hermana es mayor que yo.
examples.translation: My sister is older than me.

examples.original: Tengo dos primos y una prima.
examples.translation: I have two cousins (male) and one cousin (female).

examples.note: familia numerosa = large family (with many children).

title: Verbos reflexivos básicos / Basic reflexive verbs
bridge: In your language, you say 'I call myself John' or 'I wash myself', but reflexive verbs are less common. In the target language, reflexive verbs are very common for daily routines: 'levantarse' (to get up), 'acostarse' (to go to bed), 'ducharse' (to shower), 'vestirse' (to get dressed), 'llamarse' (to be called). The reflexive pronoun (me, te, se, nos, os, se) goes before the conjugated verb. Your typical mistake is placing the pronoun after the verb (like in English 'I wash myself' → 'Yo lavo me' instead of 'Yo me lavo'), or forgetting to use the pronoun altogether ('Yo levanto' instead of 'Yo me levanto'). Also, you might confuse when a verb is reflexive vs. non-reflexive (e.g., 'lavar' vs. 'lavarse').
rule: Reflexive verbs are formed with a reflexive pronoun that matches the subject: me (I), te (you, informal), se (he/she/it/you formal), nos (we), os (you all, informal in Spain), se (they/you all formal). The pronoun is placed BEFORE the conjugated verb. Example: 'Me levanto a las siete' (I get up at seven). When the verb is used in infinitive after another verb, the pronoun can be attached to the end of the infinitive or placed before the conjugated verb: 'Quiero levantarme' or 'Me quiero levantar' (I want to get up). Common reflexive verbs for daily routine: levantarse (to get up), acostarse (to go to bed), ducharse (to shower), bañarse (to bathe), vestirse (to get dressed), despertarse (to wake up), llamarse (to be called).

common_error.explanation: You omitted the reflexive pronoun 'me'. In the target language, when you wash your own hands, you need the reflexive verb 'lavarse'. Without the pronoun, 'lavar' means to wash something else. So 'Yo lavo las manos' sounds like 'I wash (someone else's) hands'. The correct form is 'Yo me lavo las manos' (I wash my hands).

examples.translation: My name is Ana (I call myself Ana).

examples.original: Ella se ducha por la mañana.

examples.translation: I want to get up early.
examples.note: Pronoun attached to the end of infinitive.

examples.translation: I want to get up early (alternative).

title: Preguntar y decir la hora, fechas y precios / Asking and telling time, dates and prices
bridge: In English, you ask 'What time is it?' and answer 'It's three o'clock'. In the target language, you use '¿Qué hora es?' (What hour is it?) and answer 'Es la una' (It's one o'clock) for 1:00, or 'Son las dos' (It's two o'clock) for other hours. For dates, you ask '¿Qué fecha es hoy?' or '¿A cuántos estamos?' and answer using 'Es el + number + de + month' (Es el 5 de mayo). For prices, you ask '¿Cuánto cuesta?' (How much does it cost?) and answer 'Cuesta 10 euros'. Your most common mistake is using 'Es la una' for all hours (instead of 'Son las...' for plural), and forgetting to say 'y' (and) or 'menos' (less) for minutes. Also, you might confuse '¿Cuánto cuesta?' (singular) with '¿Cuánto cuestan?' (plural).
rule: Time: ¿Qué hora es? – Es la una (1:00), Son las dos/tres... (2:00,3:00...). For minutes past the hour: Son las dos y diez (2:10). For half past: y media. For quarter past: y cuarto. For minutes to the hour: Son las tres menos veinte (2:40). For noon: mediodía; midnight: medianoche. Dates: ¿Qué fecha es hoy? – Hoy es 15 de mayo. Or: Estamos a 15 de mayo. Days of week: lunes, martes, miércoles, jueves, viernes, sábado, domingo. Months: enero, febrero, marzo, abril, mayo, junio, julio, agosto, septiembre, octubre, noviembre, diciembre. Prices: ¿Cuánto cuesta esto? – Cuesta 10 euros. ¿Cuánto cuestan estos zapatos? – Cuestan 50 euros. Numbers 1-1000 are essential.

common_error.wrong: Es la dos de la tarde.
common_error.explanation: You used 'es' (singular) with 'dos' (plural). In the target language, the verb 'ser' agrees with the hour number: 'es' for 1:00 (es la una), 'son' for all other hours (son las dos, son las tres). This is a very common mistake for English speakers.
common_error.ejemplo_1: Son las dos de la tarde.

examples.translation: What time is it? – It's one o'clock.
How much does this book cost? – It costs 15 euros.

examples.original: ¿Cuánto cuestan las manzanas? – Cuestan 2 euros el kilo.
examples.translation: How much are the apples? – They cost 2 euros per kilo.

translations.en: asking and telling time, dates and prices

title: La casa / The house
bridge: In English, we say 'house, apartment, rooms (living room, bedroom, kitchen, bathroom), furniture (table, chair, bed, closet, sofa), appliances (refrigerator, washing machine, oven)'. In Spanish, it's similar: 'casa, piso, salón (or sala), dormitorio (or habitación), cocina, baño, comedor, garaje, jardín, mesa, silla, cama, armario, sofá, nevera (or frigorífico), lavadora, horno'. The main difficulties for English speakers are: 1) The gender of nouns: 'el baño' (masculine) vs 'la cocina' (feminine) – English speakers often use the wrong article because they think of the English word's gender (e.g., 'bathroom' is neuter in English, so they might say 'la baño' incorrectly). 2) The position of adjectives: in English we say 'big house', but in Spanish it's 'casa grande' (adjective after noun). 3) The use of 'en' for location (e.g., 'en la cocina') vs 'en' also used for 'in' – it's similar to English, but English speakers sometimes confuse 'sobre' (on) with 'en' (in/on).
rule: Rooms: salón (or sala), cocina, dormitorio (or habitación), baño, comedor, garaje, jardín, pasillo. Furniture: mesa, silla, cama, armario, sofá, estantería, escritorio. Appliances: nevera (or frigorífico), lavadora, horno, microondas, lavavajillas. Gender: 'el baño' (m), 'la cocina' (f), 'el dormitorio' (m), 'la habitación' (f). For location, use 'estar' (not 'ser') + 'en' (e.g., 'La mesa está en la cocina').

common_error.explanation: The error is using the feminine article 'la' with 'baño', which is masculine. English speakers don't have grammatical gender for inanimate objects, so they often guess the gender based on the English word or random chance. 'Baño' is masculine, so it's 'el baño'.

examples.original: La mesa está en el comedor.
examples.translation: The table is in the dining room.

examples.original: Pongo la ropa en la lavadora.
examples.translation: I put the clothes in the washing machine.

examples.original: La nevera está llena de comida.
examples.translation: The refrigerator is full of food.

examples.original: Vivo en un piso con jardín.
examples.translation: I live in an apartment with a garden.

title: Pronombres personales sujeto / Subject personal pronouns
bridge: In English, you have subject pronouns: I, you, he, she, it, we, they. In the target language, there are more: yo (I), tú (you, informal singular), vos (you, informal singular – used in some regions), usted (you, formal singular), él (he), ella (she), nosotros/nosotras (we, masculine/feminine), vosotros/vosotras (you all, informal plural – used mainly in Spain), ustedes (you all, formal plural or general plural in Americas), ellos (they, masculine), ellas (they, feminine). The most common mistake for English speakers is using the subject pronoun too often. In the target language, the verb ending already indicates the subject, so pronouns are often omitted (except for emphasis or clarity). You also tend to confuse 'tú' and 'usted' (formal vs. informal), and you may use 'tú' with the wrong verb form (e.g., 'tú habla' instead of 'tú hablas'). Another common error is using 'ello' for 'it' – but 'ello' is rarely used; you usually omit the subject pronoun for 'it' altogether.
rule: Subject pronouns in the target language: yo (I), tú (you, informal singular), vos (you, informal singular – regional), usted (you, formal singular), él (he), ella (she), nosotros/nosotras (we), vosotros/vosotras (you all, informal plural – Spain), ustedes (you all, formal/plural), ellos/ellas (they). The verb conjugation indicates the subject, so subject pronouns are optional and usually omitted unless you want to emphasize the subject or avoid ambiguity. Example: 'Hablo español' (I speak Spanish) is fine; 'Yo hablo español' adds emphasis. For 'it', there is no separate pronoun for things; you just omit the subject or use 'él/ella' if referring to a specific noun (e.g., '¿Dónde está el libro? Él está en la mesa' is possible but rare; better: 'Está en la mesa').

common_error.explanation: You used the verb form 'habla' (which is for él/ella/usted) with the subject 'tú'. The correct form for 'tú' is 'hablas'. In English, the verb 'speak' doesn't change for 'you' (you speak), so English speakers often forget to use the correct conjugation for 'tú'. Remember: tú → hablas, él/ella/usted → habla.

examples.note: Yo = I (often omitted: 'Soy Ana' is enough).

examples.translation: We live here (masculine or mixed group).
You all are from Spain (informal plural, used in Spain).
They work a lot (masculine or mixed).
What's your name? – My name is Ana.
examples.note: No 'yo' needed; the verb 'llamo' implies the subject.

examples.original: Ella habla inglés, pero yo hablo español.
examples.translation: She speaks English, but I speak Spanish.

title: Hablar de rutinas diarias y acciones habituales / Talking about daily routines and habitual actions
bridge: In English, you talk about your daily routine using the present simple: 'I wake up at 7, then I have breakfast, and I go to work'. In the target language, you also use the present tense (presente de indicativo) to describe habitual actions. You will need reflexive verbs for many routine actions: 'levantarse' (to get up), 'ducharse' (to shower), 'vestirse' (to get dressed), 'acostarse' (to go to bed). The most common mistake for English speakers is forgetting the reflexive pronoun (e.g., 'Levanto a las 7' instead of 'Me levanto a las 7') or using the wrong reflexive pronoun. Also, you might place time expressions incorrectly or use the present continuous instead of the simple present for routines.
rule: Use present tense (regular and irregular verbs) to describe daily routines. For reflexive verbs, place the reflexive pronoun (me, te, se, nos, os, se) before the conjugated verb. Common time expressions: por la mañana (in the morning), por la tarde (in the afternoon), por la noche (at night), todos los días (every day), siempre (always), nunca (never), a veces (sometimes). Example sequence: Me levanto a las siete. Luego me ducho y me visto. Después desayuno. A las ocho salgo de casa. Work and study verbs: trabajar, estudiar, ir al trabajo/escuela, volver a casa, cenar, ver la tele, acostarse. To ask about someone's routine: ¿Qué haces por la mañana? ¿A qué hora te levantas?

common_error.explanation: You forgot the reflexive pronoun 'me'. The verb 'levantarse' (to get up) requires the reflexive pronoun. Without it, 'levantar' means 'to lift' something else. For daily routines, always use the reflexive form with the correct pronoun.
common_error.ejemplo_1: Yo me levanto a las siete.

examples.original: Ella se ducha por la mañana.
Primero me levanto, luego me ducho.
examples.translation: First I get up, then I shower.

examples.original: Después desayuno y salgo de casa.
examples.translation: Afterwards I have breakfast and leave home.

examples.original: Por la noche ceno y veo la televisión.
examples.translation: At night I have dinner and watch TV.
What time do you get up?
examples.note: Question with 'a qué hora' and reflexive.

examples.translation: What do you normally have for breakfast?
How many hours do you sleep?

title: La ciudad / The city
bridge: In English, we say 'street, square, supermarket, pharmacy, bank, park, cinema, restaurant, hospital, school, train station, airport'. In Spanish, it's similar: 'calle, plaza, supermercado, farmacia, banco, parque, cine, restaurante, hospital, escuela, estación, aeropuerto'. The main difficulties for English speakers are: 1) The word 'cine' (cinema) vs 'cinema' – English speakers often say 'cinema' because it's the English word, but in Spanish it's 'cine'. 2) The gender of 'estación' (feminine) and 'aeropuerto' (masculine) – English speakers may use the wrong article. 3) The preposition 'a' for direction ('voy al cine') vs 'en' for location ('estoy en el cine') – English speakers may confuse 'to' and 'in'. Also, the use of 'estar' for location ('el banco está en la calle Mayor') – English speakers might use 'ser' incorrectly because in English we say 'the bank is' with 'to be'.
rule: Places: calle (f), plaza (f), supermercado (m), farmacia (f), banco (m), parque (m), cine (m), restaurante (m), hospital (m), escuela (f), iglesia (f), museo (m), estación (f), aeropuerto (m). Use 'estar' for location (e.g., '¿Dónde está el banco?'). Use 'ir a' for going to a place (e.g., 'Voy al cine').

common_error.wrong: Voy al cinema con mis amigos.
common_error.explanation: The error is using 'cinema' instead of 'cine'. In Spanish, the place is called 'cine' (from 'cinematógrafo'), not 'cinema'. English speakers often use the English word because it's more familiar, but it's incorrect in Spanish.
common_error.ejemplo_1: Voy al cine con mis amigos.

examples.original: Voy al supermercado a comprar pan.
examples.translation: I go to the supermarket to buy bread.

examples.original: El hospital está cerca de la plaza.
examples.translation: The hospital is near the square.

examples.original: La farmacia está a la derecha del banco.
examples.translation: The pharmacy is to the right of the bank.

examples.original: El cine está en la calle Mayor.
examples.translation: The cinema is on Main Street.

title: Demostrativos / Demonstratives
bridge: In English, you have 'this' (near me), 'that' (near you or far), 'these' (plural near), 'those' (plural far). In the target language, there is a three‑distance system: 'este' (this – near me), 'ese' (that – near you, or less near), 'aquel' (that – far from both). They also have feminine and plural forms. The most common mistake for English speakers is using 'este' for everything because English has only two distances. You also confuse 'ese' with 'aquel' and forget to match the gender of the noun (e.g., 'esta casa' is feminine, but you might say 'este casa'). Another mistake is using the neutral forms 'esto', 'eso', 'aquello' incorrectly (they are used for unspecified things or ideas, not with nouns).
rule: Demonstratives agree in gender and number with the noun they modify. Masculine singular: este (this), ese (that), aquel (that far). Feminine singular: esta (this), esa (that), aquella (that far). Masculine plural: estos (these), esos (those), aquellos (those far). Feminine plural: estas (these), esas (those), aquellas (those far). Neutral forms (no noun, for ideas or unknown things): esto (this thing), eso (that thing), aquello (that thing far). Use 'este' for things close to the speaker, 'ese' for things close to the listener or not very near, 'aquel' for things far from both.

common_error.explanation: You used the masculine form 'este' with the feminine noun 'casa'. Demonstratives must match the gender of the noun. Since 'casa' is feminine, you need 'esta'. This is a common error because English does not have grammatical gender, so you are not used to changing the form of 'this/that'.

examples.translation: That mountain (over there) is very high.

title: Describir el tiempo atmosférico / Describing the weather
bridge: In English, you say 'It's sunny', 'It's raining', 'It's cold'. In the target language, you use the verb 'hacer' (to do/make) for many weather expressions: 'hace sol' (it's sunny), 'hace calor' (it's hot), 'hace frío' (it's cold), 'hace viento' (it's windy). For rain, snow, and other phenomena, you use the verb 'llover' (to rain) and 'nevar' (to snow) conjugated in the third person singular: 'llueve', 'nieva'. You can also use 'está' + adjective: 'está nublado' (cloudy), 'está despejado' (clear). The most common mistake for English speakers is using 'es' instead of 'hace' for weather (e.g., 'Es calor' instead of 'Hace calor') or using the wrong verb 'tener' (e.g., 'Tiene calor' – which means 'he/she is hot').
rule: Use 'hacer' for general conditions: Hace sol (sunny), hace calor (hot), hace frío (cold), hace viento (windy), hace buen tiempo (nice weather), hace mal tiempo (bad weather). Use 'llover' and 'nevar' as impersonal verbs: Llueve (it's raining), Llueve mucho, Nieva (it's snowing). Use 'estar' for temporary states: Está nublado (cloudy), Está despejado (clear), Está lloviendo (it's raining – progressive). Questions: ¿Qué tiempo hace? (What's the weather like?). ¿Hace frío? (Is it cold?). Also: Hay niebla (foggy), Hay tormenta (stormy).

common_error.explanation: You used the verb 'ser' (es) to describe hot weather. In the target language, weather expressions like 'hot', 'cold', 'windy', 'sunny' use the verb 'hacer' (to do/make). 'Hace calor' literally means 'it makes heat'. This is a fixed expression; never use 'ser' or 'estar' for these.

examples.translation: It rains a lot in April.

examples.original: En invierno hace frío y nieva.
examples.translation: In winter it's cold and it snows.
There's a storm, we can't go out.

title: Profesiones / Professions
bridge: In English, we say 'teacher, doctor, nurse, engineer, student, cook, driver, lawyer, architect, journalist, firefighter, police officer, pilot, flight attendant'. In Spanish, it's very similar: 'profesor, médico, enfermero, ingeniero, estudiante, cocinero, conductor, abogado, arquitecto, periodista, bombero, policía, piloto, azafato/azafata'. The main differences are: 1) In Spanish, professions are not preceded by an article after the verb 'ser' (e.g., 'Soy profesor' – not 'Soy un profesor'). English speakers often add the article because in English we say 'I am a teacher'. 2) Many professions change to feminine by changing -o to -a (e.g., 'enfermero/enfermera'), but some are invariable (e.g., 'periodista', 'policía'). 3) The word 'conductor' is used for 'driver', not 'motorista' (which is less common). 4) English speakers may confuse 'abogado' with 'advocate' (false friend) – 'abogado' means 'lawyer'.
rule: Professions: profesor, profesora (teacher), médico, médica (doctor), enfermero, enfermera (nurse), ingeniero, ingeniera (engineer), estudiante (student, invariable), cocinero, cocinera (cook), conductor, conductora (driver), abogado, abogada (lawyer), arquitecto, arquitecta (architect), periodista (journalist, invariable), bombero, bombera (firefighter), policía (police officer, invariable), piloto (pilot, invariable), azafato, azafata (flight attendant). No article after 'ser': 'Soy profesor' (not 'Soy un profesor').

common_error.explanation: The error is adding the indefinite article 'un' before the profession after 'ser'. In English, we say 'I am a teacher', but in Spanish, the article is omitted when stating a profession with the verb 'ser'. This is a very common mistake for English speakers.

examples.translation: What do you do? - I'm a student.

examples.original: El periodista trabaja en la radio.
examples.translation: The journalist works on the radio.
examples.note: Invariable in gender ('periodista' is both masculine and feminine).

title: Posesivos / Possessives (unstressed forms)
bridge: In English, you have possessive adjectives: my, your, his, her, its, our, their. In the target language, there are two sets: unstressed (short) forms that go before the noun, and stressed (long) forms that go after. For A1, we focus on unstressed forms: mi (my), tu (your, informal singular), su (his, her, its, your formal, their), nuestro/a (our), vuestro/a (your plural informal – Spain), su (your plural formal/their). The most common mistake for English speakers is using 'su' for everything (because it can mean his, her, their, your) and forgetting to match 'nuestro/vuestro' in gender and number. Also, you tend to use the definite article with possessives (e.g., 'el mi libro' instead of 'mi libro'), which is incorrect. Another mistake is confusing 'tu' (your) with 'tú' (you, subject pronoun).
rule: Unstressed possessive adjectives go BEFORE the noun and agree in number with the noun (except for mi, tu, su which do not change for number? Actually mi/mis, tu/tus, su/sus do change for plural: mi libro (my book), mis libros (my books). Nuestro, vuestro change for gender and number: nuestro/nuestra/nuestros/nuestras. Examples: mi casa (my house), mis casas (my houses). tu casa (your house), tus casas (your houses). su casa (his/her/their/your house), sus casas (his/her/their/your houses). nuestro perro (our dog), nuestra perra, nuestros perros, nuestras perras. vuestro hermano (your brother), vuestra hermana, vuestros hermanos, vuestras hermanas. Note: 'su' can be ambiguous; you can clarify by adding 'de él', 'de ella', 'de usted', 'de ellos' after the noun.

common_error.explanation: You added the definite article 'el' before the possessive 'mi'. In the target language, unstressed possessives are used directly before the noun without an article. 'El mi libro' is incorrect; it should be 'mi libro'. This is a common error because in English you can say 'the my book'? No, you don't. Actually English doesn't use article with possessives, so this error might come from other languages. But English speakers sometimes overcompensate. The rule: never use an article with unstressed possessives.

title: Pedir y dar direcciones / Asking for and giving directions
bridge: In English, you ask 'Where is the bank?' and give directions: 'Go straight, turn left, it's on the corner'. In the target language, you use '¿Dónde está...?' or '¿Cómo se va a...?' (How do you get to...?). For directions, use the imperative informal (tú) for common verbs: 'sigue' (continue), 'gira' (turn), 'cruza' (cross). Also use 'está' for location: 'está a la izquierda', 'está a la derecha', 'está cerca/lejos'. Your most common mistake is using the infinitive instead of the imperative (e.g., 'Seguir recto' instead of 'Sigue recto'), or translating word-for-word from English (e.g., 'Ve recto' instead of 'Sigue recto'). Also, you may forget the personal 'a' when referring to a person (not needed for places).
rule: To ask: ¿Dónde está el banco? (Where is the bank?) ¿Cómo se va a la estación? (How do you get to the station?). To give directions: Sigue todo recto (Go straight). Gira a la izquierda/derecha (Turn left/right). Cruza la calle (Cross the street). Toma la primera calle a la derecha (Take the first street on the right). Location phrases: Está a la izquierda (on the left), a la derecha (on the right), en la esquina (on the corner), cerca (near), lejos (far), al lado de (next to), enfrente de (opposite). Use imperative 'tú' for informal directions. Common verbs in imperative: sigue (seguir), gira (girar), cruza (cruzar), toma (tomar), camina (caminar).

common_error.explanation: You used the infinitive 'seguir' instead of the imperative 'sigue'. When giving directions directly to someone, use the imperative form (tú). The infinitive is used in impersonal instructions (like on signs) or after 'para', but not when directly telling someone what to do. 'Sigue recto' is the correct command.

examples.original: ¿Cómo se va a la plaza mayor?
examples.translation: How do you get to the main square?

examples.original: Está a la derecha, en la esquina.
examples.translation: It's on the right, on the corner.

examples.original: Cruza la calle y sigue dos cuadras.
examples.translation: Cross the street and go two blocks.

examples.original: Toma la segunda calle a la derecha.
examples.translation: Take the second street on the right.

examples.original: El banco está al lado del supermercado.
examples.translation: The bank is next to the supermarket.

title: Ropa y accesorios / Clothing and accessories
bridge: In English, we say 'shirt, pants, dress, shoes, jacket, coat, sweater, socks, hat, belt, bag, tie, glasses, watch, bracelet'. In Spanish, it's similar: 'camisa, pantalón, vestido, zapatos, chaqueta, abrigo, jersey, calcetines, sombrero, cinturón, bolso, corbata, gafas, reloj, pulsera'. The main difficulties for English speakers are: 1) The gender of some words: 'el pantalón' (masculine) – English speakers might say 'la pantalón' because 'pants' are plural in English. 2) The accent on 'pantalón' and 'cinturón' (they are written with an accent). 3) The plural of 'calcetín' is 'calcetines' – English speakers might say 'calcetines' correctly but may forget the accent. 4) 'Gafas' is always plural in Spanish, even though in English we say 'glasses' (also plural). 5) The word 'bolso' for 'bag' vs 'bolsa' (which means 'plastic bag') – English speakers might confuse them.
rule: Clothing: camisa (f), pantalón (m), vestido (m), falda (f), chaqueta (f), abrigo (m), jersey (m), camiseta (f), calcetines (m.pl), zapatos (m.pl), botas (f.pl). Accessories: sombrero (m), cinturón (m), bolso (m), corbata (f), gafas (f.pl), reloj (m), pulsera (f). For wearing something, use 'llevar' (e.g., 'Llevo una chaqueta negra').

common_error.explanation: The error is missing the accent on 'pantalón'. In Spanish, words ending in -n, -s, or a vowel that are stressed on the last syllable must have an accent. 'Pantalón' is stressed on the 'o', so it needs an accent. English speakers often forget accents because they don't exist in English.

examples.translation: I want to try on this shirt.
Do you have this skirt in size M?

title: Preposiciones básicas / Basic prepositions
bridge: In English, you have prepositions like 'to', 'with', 'of', 'in', 'for', 'by', 'without', 'on'. In the target language, there are similar prepositions: 'a' (to), 'con' (with), 'de' (of/from), 'en' (in/on/at), 'para' (for – purpose/destination), 'por' (for – cause/exchange/through), 'sin' (without), 'sobre' (on/over/about). The most common mistakes for English speakers are confusing 'por' and 'para' (both can mean 'for'), and using 'en' where English uses 'on' or 'at' (which is often correct but sometimes not, e.g., 'Estoy en la mesa' vs. 'Estoy en la silla' – both are fine, but 'en' is used more broadly). Also, you might translate the English 'to' as 'a' when it should be 'para' or nothing. And you often forget the personal 'a' before a direct object that is a specific person (e.g., 'Veo a Juan' not 'Veo Juan'). For A1, focus on basic uses: 'a' for motion towards, 'de' for possession or origin, 'en' for location, 'con' for company, 'sin' for absence, 'para' for destination/purpose, 'por' for cause or means.
rule: Basic prepositions: a (to, at, for movement), con (with), de (of, from), en (in, on, at), para (for – destination, purpose, recipient), por (for – cause, exchange, duration, through), sin (without), sobre (on, over, about). Examples: 'Voy a Madrid' (I go to Madrid). 'Café con leche' (coffee with milk). 'El libro de Ana' (Ana's book). 'Vivo en España' (I live in Spain). 'Este regalo es para ti' (This gift is for you). 'Gracias por tu ayuda' (Thanks for your help). 'Café sin azúcar' (coffee without sugar). 'El libro está sobre la mesa' (The book is on the table). Important: Use personal 'a' before a direct object that is a specific person: 'Veo a Juan' (I see Juan), not 'Veo Juan'. This does not exist in English.

common_error.explanation: You used 'para' to mean 'for' in thanks, but the correct preposition is 'por'. In English, both 'thank you for' and 'this gift is for you' use 'for'. In the target language, 'para' is used for purpose, destination, or recipient (e.g., 'un regalo para ti'), while 'por' is used for cause, motive, or exchange (e.g., 'gracias por tu ayuda', 'lo compré por 10 euros'). This distinction is difficult for English speakers.

levels.label: a / de / en
levels.translation: to / of, from / in, on, at

title: Realizar compras básicas / Basic shopping
bridge: In English, you say 'How much is it?', 'I would like...', 'Do you have...?'. In the target language, you will use '¿Cuánto cuesta?' (How much is it?), '¿Cuánto cuestan?' (How much are they?), 'Quisiera...' (I would like...), '¿Tiene...?' (Do you have...?). You also need numbers, colors, and sizes. The most common mistake for English speakers is using the verb 'costar' incorrectly ('Cuesta' is fine, but forgetting the plural 'cuestan'), or using 'quiero' (I want) instead of the more polite 'quisiera' (I would like). Another mistake is using 'un/una' with currency (e.g., 'diez un euros').
rule: Key phrases: ¿Cuánto cuesta esto? (How much is this?) – Cuesta [number] euros. ¿Cuánto cuestan estos? (How much are these?) – Cuestan [number] euros. Quisiera comprar... (I would like to buy...). ¿Tiene/tienes...? (Do you have...?). ¿Me puede dar...? (Can you give me...?). Size and color: ¿De qué talla? (What size?), ¿De qué color? (What color?). For payment: ¿Aceptan tarjeta? (Do you take card?), En efectivo (in cash). Numbers 1-1000 are essential. Remember: 'cuesta' for singular items, 'cuestan' for plural. Use 'quisiera' (conditional form of 'querer') for polite requests, not 'quiero'.

common_error.wrong: ¿Cuánto cuesta los zapatos? – Cuesta cincuenta euros.
common_error.explanation: You used the singular 'cuesta' with the plural noun 'zapatos' (shoes). The verb 'costar' must agree with the subject: 'cuesta' for singular, 'cuestan' for plural. The correct question is '¿Cuánto cuestan los zapatos?' and the answer is 'Cuestan cincuenta euros'.
common_error.ejemplo_1: ¿Cuánto cuestan los zapatos? – Cuestan cincuenta euros.

examples.translation: They cost two euros per kilo.
I would like to buy a shirt.
Do you have this shirt in blue?

title: Comida y bebida / Food and drink
bridge: In English, we say 'bread, rice, pasta, meat, fish, fruit, vegetables, eggs, cheese, yogurt' and 'water, juice, coffee, tea, milk, beer, wine'. In Spanish, it's similar: 'pan, arroz, pasta, carne, pescado, frutas, verduras, huevos, queso, yogur' and 'agua, zumo (or jugo), café, té, leche, cerveza, vino'. The main difficulties for English speakers are: 1) The gender of 'agua' (feminine, but uses 'el' in singular because it starts with stressed 'a' – 'el agua', but 'las aguas'). English speakers often say 'la agua' (incorrect). 2) 'Zumo' is used in Spain, 'jugo' in Latin America – English speakers might not know which one to use. 3) The use of the partitive article doesn't exist in Spanish: in English we say 'I drink water', in Spanish it's 'Bebo agua' (no article). 4) Plural forms of food items: 'frutas' vs 'fruta' (fruit as a category is 'fruta', but plural for multiple pieces).
rule: Food: pan (m), arroz (m), pasta (f), carne (f), pescado (m), fruta (f), verdura (f), huevo (m), queso (m), yogur (m). Drinks: agua (f, but 'el agua' and 'un agua'), zumo (m, Spain) / jugo (m, Latin America), café (m), té (m), leche (f), cerveza (f), vino (m). For general statements, use no article: 'Como pan' (I eat bread), 'Bebo agua' (I drink water). For preferences, use the definite article: 'Me gusta el pan' (I like bread).

common_error.explanation: The error is using the partitive article 'del' (de + el) which doesn't exist in Spanish. In English, we say 'I drink water' without an article. In Spanish, you also don't use an article for general amounts. The partitive article is not used in Spanish for quantities of food or drink.

examples.translation: I buy a kilo of apples.
I want a coffee with milk.

examples.original: La paella es un plato típico español.
examples.translation: Paella is a typical Spanish dish.

title: Adverbios de lugar y tiempo / Adverbs of place and time
bridge: In English, you have adverbs like 'here', 'there', 'today', 'tomorrow', 'yesterday', 'now', 'then', 'before', 'after'. In the target language, they are very similar: 'aquí' (here), 'ahí/allí' (there), 'hoy' (today), 'mañana' (tomorrow), 'ayer' (yesterday), 'ahora' (now), 'entonces' (then), 'antes' (before), 'después' (after). The most common mistake for English speakers is confusing 'aquí', 'ahí', and 'allí' because English has only 'here' and 'there'. Also, you may place the adverb in the wrong position. In the target language, adverbs of time often go at the beginning or end of the sentence, but not between the verb and the object as often as in English ('I eat now' → 'Como ahora' is fine, but 'Ahora como' is also fine). Another common error is using 'hoy' as an adjective (e.g., 'el día hoy' instead of 'el día de hoy' or just 'hoy'). Also, note that 'mañana' means both 'tomorrow' and 'morning', so context is key.
rule: Adverbs of place: aquí (here – near speaker), ahí (there – near listener), allí (there – far from both). Also: cerca (near), lejos (far). Adverbs of time: hoy (today), mañana (tomorrow), ayer (yesterday), ahora (now), entonces (then), antes (before), después (after), siempre (always), nunca (never), a veces (sometimes). Position: They usually go before or after the verb, but not between the verb and the object when the object is a noun. Examples: 'Ahora como' or 'Como ahora' (I eat now). 'Hoy voy al cine' (Today I go to the movies). 'Vivo aquí' (I live here). Note: 'Mañana' can be ambiguous: 'mañana por la mañana' means 'tomorrow morning'.

common_error.explanation: The sentence is actually correct, but the typical mistake is placing adverbs incorrectly in longer sentences. A more common error is using 'hoy' with an article: 'el hoy' is wrong. For A1, the main error is confusing 'aquí', 'ahí', 'allí' because English only distinguishes two distances. For example, pointing to something near the listener: you should say 'ahí', but English speakers say 'there' and use 'allí' for both. So they might say 'allí' for something near the listener, when 'ahí' would be more precise.
common_error.ejemplo_1: Ahora voy a la playa / Voy a la playa ahora.

examples.translation: Tomorrow I go to the doctor.

examples.original: Antes de comer, lavo las manos.
examples.translation: Before eating, I wash my hands.

examples.original: Después de clase, voy a casa.

title: Ordenar en un bar o restaurante / Ordering in a bar or restaurant
bridge: In English, you say 'I would like a coffee', 'Can I have the menu?', 'The bill, please'. In the target language, you use 'Quisiera...' (I would like) or '¿Me trae...?' (Can you bring me?), '¿Puedo ver el menú?' (Can I see the menu?), 'La cuenta, por favor' (The bill, please). The most common mistake for English speakers is using 'quiero' (I want) which can sound rude, instead of 'quisiera' or 'me gustaría'. Also, forgetting to use the personal 'a' when ordering for someone else (e.g., 'Para mi amigo' is fine, but 'Trae a mi amigo' is not needed). Another mistake is mispronouncing food items.
rule: Polite requests: Quisiera un café, por favor (I would like a coffee, please). Me gustaría pedir una ensalada (I would like to order a salad). ¿Me puede traer la carta? (Can you bring me the menu?). To order for someone: Para mí, una paella (For me, a paella). Para ella, una ensalada (For her, a salad). Asking questions: ¿Qué me recomienda? (What do you recommend?). ¿Tienen menú del día? (Do you have a set menu?). Payment: La cuenta, por favor. ¿Puedo pagar con tarjeta? (Can I pay by card?). Vocabulary: camarero/a (waiter), la carta (menu), el plato (dish), la bebida (drink), el postre (dessert), la propina (tip).

common_error.explanation: You used 'quiero' (I want), which is direct and can sound impolite or demanding in a restaurant setting. In the target language, it is more polite to use 'quisiera' (I would like) or 'me gustaría' (I would like). Always add 'por favor' (please) to be courteous.

examples.translation: I would like a beer, please.
Can you bring me the menu, please?
What is the soup made of?

examples.original: Creo que hay un error en la cuenta.
examples.translation: I think there is a mistake in the bill.

translations.en: ordering in a bar or restaurant

title: Animales domésticos y de granja / Pets and farm animals
bridge: In English, we say 'dog, cat, cow, horse, chicken, pig, sheep, duck, rabbit, goat'. In Spanish, it's similar: 'perro, gato, vaca, caballo, gallina, cerdo, oveja, pato, conejo, cabra'. The main difficulties for English speakers are: 1) The difference between 'perro' (dog) and 'cachorro' (puppy). English speakers often use 'puppy' for a young dog, but in Spanish, 'cachorro' means 'puppy' (or cub), not the adult dog. 2) The gender of some animals: 'el caballo' (horse) is masculine, even though in English it's neuter. 3) The plural forms: 'los perros' (the dogs) – English speakers might forget to add the -s. 4) Some false friends: 'cerdo' (pig) is similar to 'cider'? No, but 'puerco' is also used in some regions. The most common error is using 'cachorro' for an adult dog.
rule: Pets: perro (m), gato (m), conejo (m), hámster (m), pez (m). Farm animals: vaca (f), caballo (m), oveja (f), cerdo (m), gallina (f), pato (m), cabra (f), burro (m). For the species, we often use the masculine gender (e.g., 'el perro' for the species), but for the female animal, we use the feminine form ('la perra' for a female dog).

common_error.explanation: The error is using 'cachorro' (puppy) to describe an adult dog. In Spanish, 'cachorro' means 'puppy' or 'cub' (young animal). For an adult dog, you should say 'perro' (or 'perra' for a female dog). English speakers may confuse this because they think 'cachorro' sounds similar to 'cocker' or they associate it with any dog, but it specifically means a young one.

examples.original: El perro es grande y marrón.
examples.translation: The dog is big and brown.

examples.original: Mi gato duerme en la cama.
examples.translation: My cat sleeps on the bed.

examples.original: Tengo un perro y dos gatos.
examples.translation: I have a dog and two cats.

title: Oraciones interrogativas / Interrogative sentences
bridge: In English, you form questions by inverting the subject and verb (You are → Are you?) or using auxiliary verbs (Do you like?). In the target language, questions can be formed by simply changing the intonation (rising at the end) without changing word order. You can also invert the subject and verb, but inversion is less common in everyday speech. The most common mistake for English speakers is using the English auxiliary 'do/does' in Spanish questions (e.g., '¿Haces tú?' is correct as inversion, but they might say '¿Do you like?' calque). Another mistake is placing the subject before the verb in an inverted question (e.g., '¿Tú hablas español?' is correct, but that's not inversion; inversion would be '¿Hablas tú español?'. Both are fine. The real error is using the English question word order or forgetting the initial question mark (¿). Also, confusion between '¿Qué?' and '¿Cuál?' (what vs. which), and using '¿Cómo?' (how) for 'what?' in some contexts.
rule: Question formation: 1) Intonation only: 'Tú hablas español?' (rising intonation). 2) Inversion: '¿Hablas tú español?' (verb before subject). 3) Question words: ¿Qué? (what), ¿Quién? (who), ¿Cómo? (how), ¿Cuándo? (when), ¿Dónde? (where), ¿Por qué? (why), ¿Cuál? (which one), ¿Cuánto? (how much/many). These always carry an accent mark. Question words go at the beginning of the sentence. Example: '¿Dónde está la estación?' (Where is the station?). Note: In Spanish, you do not need an auxiliary verb like 'do' or 'does'. Just use the question word and the verb. Also, remember the inverted question mark (¿) at the beginning.

common_error.explanation: You placed the subject 'tú' between the question word and the verb. In Spanish, the typical order is question word + verb + subject, or just question word + verb (subject omitted). '¿Qué tu quieres?' is a direct translation of English 'What do you want?' word order, which is incorrect. The correct order is '¿Qué quieres?' or '¿Qué quieres tú?' (with subject after verb).

examples.translation: What's your name? (How do you call yourself?)

title: Expresar acuerdo y desacuerdo simple / Expressing simple agreement and disagreement
bridge: In English, you say 'I agree', 'You're right', 'I don't agree', 'I think so', 'I don't think so'. In the target language, you use 'Estoy de acuerdo' (I agree), 'No estoy de acuerdo' (I don't agree), 'Tienes razón' (You're right), 'No tienes razón' (You're wrong), 'Creo que sí' (I think so), 'Creo que no' (I don't think so). The most common mistake for English speakers is using 'Acuerdo' alone (which means 'agreement' as a noun) instead of 'Estoy de acuerdo'. Also, confusing 'de acuerdo' (agreed) with 'acuerdo' (agreement) and forgetting the verb 'estar'. Another mistake is directly translating 'I think so' as 'Pienso así' instead of 'Creo que sí'.
rule: To agree: Estoy de acuerdo (I agree). Tienes razón (You're right). Claro (Of course). Exacto (Exactly). To disagree: No estoy de acuerdo (I don't agree). No tienes razón (You're wrong). Eso no es cierto (That's not true). Para mí, no (Not for me). To express opinion: Creo que sí (I think so). Creo que no (I don't think so). Pienso que... (I think that...). En mi opinión... (In my opinion...). Use 'estoy de acuerdo' (not 'soy de acuerdo' or 'acuerdo' alone). 'De acuerdo' can also be used alone as an interjection: '¡De acuerdo!' (Agreed!).

common_error.explanation: You used 'acuerdo' (the noun) as if it were a verb, and incorrectly said 'con tú'. The correct phrase is 'Estoy de acuerdo contigo' (I agree with you). 'Acuerdo' alone is not a verb. Alternatively, you can simply say 'De acuerdo' to mean 'Agreed'.

examples.translation: I think so, you should come.
examples.note: 'Creo que sí' to agree with a suggestion.

examples.original: Pienso que el español es fácil.
En mi opinión, la película es buena.
examples.translation: In my opinion, the movie is good.

title: El cuerpo humano / The human body
bridge: In English, we say 'head, arms, legs, eyes, mouth, nose, ears, hands, feet'. In Spanish, it's similar: 'cabeza, brazos, piernas, ojos, boca, nariz, orejas, manos, pies'. The main difficulties for English speakers are: 1) The gender of some body parts: 'la mano' (hand) is feminine, but English speakers might think it's masculine because it ends in -o. 'el pie' (foot) is masculine. 'la nariz' (nose) is feminine. 2) The plural of 'pie' is 'pies' (which is also a false friend with 'pies' in English). 3) The use of 'los ojos' (eyes) and 'las orejas' (ears) – English speakers might use the singular incorrectly. 4) The expression 'me duele' (it hurts me) is used differently in Spanish: instead of 'my head hurts', you say 'me duele la cabeza' (literally, 'the head hurts me'). English speakers might say 'mi cabeza duele' (incorrect).
rule: Body parts: cabeza (f), cara (f), ojos (m), nariz (f), boca (f), orejas (f), cuello (m), hombros (m), brazos (m), manos (f), dedos (m), pecho (m), espalda (f), piernas (f), rodillas (f), pies (m). To say 'I have a headache', use 'Me duele la cabeza' (not 'Mi cabeza duele'). The possessive adjective is not used; instead, use the definite article + the body part. The indirect object pronoun (me, te, le, etc.) indicates who it hurts.

common_error.explanation: The error is using the masculine article 'el' with 'nariz', which is feminine. English speakers often guess the gender of nouns based on the word's ending, but 'nariz' ends in -z and is feminine. They might think it's masculine because it ends in a consonant, but it's actually feminine.

examples.original: Tengo el pelo largo y los ojos azules.
examples.translation: I have long hair and blue eyes.

examples.original: Lávate las manos antes de comer.
Se rompió la pierna jugando al fútbol.
examples.translation: He broke his leg playing soccer.

title: Negación / Negation
bridge: In English, you make a sentence negative by adding 'not' after the auxiliary verb ('I do not eat'), or by using words like 'no', 'nothing', 'nobody', 'never', 'neither'. In the target language, negation is simpler: you usually put 'no' directly before the verb. For example: 'No como' (I don't eat). You can also use negative words like 'nada' (nothing), 'nadie' (nobody), 'nunca' (never), 'tampoco' (neither). When these words come after the verb, the verb must still be preceded by 'no' (double negative is required). For example: 'No veo nada' (I see nothing / I don't see anything). English speakers often forget to put 'no' before the verb and just use the negative word alone ('Veo nada'), which is incorrect. Another common mistake is using 'no' multiple times incorrectly or omitting it when using 'nunca' before the verb (which does NOT require 'no').
rule: Basic negation: Place 'no' directly before the conjugated verb. Examples: 'Yo no hablo' (I don't speak), 'Ellos no comen' (They don't eat). Negative words: nada (nothing), nadie (nobody), nunca (never), tampoco (neither, either). When these words come AFTER the verb, you must also use 'no' before the verb. This is called double negative, and it is correct in the target language. Example: 'No veo nada' (I see nothing / I don't see anything). However, when the negative word comes BEFORE the verb, you do NOT use 'no'. Example: 'Nadie habla' (Nobody speaks), 'Nunca como carne' (I never eat meat). Also, 'tampoco' is used to agree with a negative statement: 'Yo no hablo inglés. – Yo tampoco' (I don't speak English. – Neither do I).

common_error.explanation: You used the negative word 'nada' without placing 'no' before the verb. In the target language, when negative words come after the verb, you must use double negation: 'no' + verb + negative word. 'Veo nada' is a direct translation from English 'I see nothing', which is incorrect. The correct form is 'No veo nada'.

examples.translation: I have nothing / I don't have anything.
I see nobody / I don't see anyone.
I never go to the movies.

examples.note: Negative word before verb – no 'no' needed.
To agree with a negative statement: 'Yo no hablo inglés. – Yo tampoco'.

title: Verbos de acción cotidianos / Everyday action verbs
bridge: In English, we say 'get up, shower, get dressed, have breakfast, go to work, work, have lunch, study, exercise, have dinner, go to bed, sleep'. In Spanish, it's similar: 'levantarse, ducharse, vestirse, desayunar, ir al trabajo, trabajar, almorzar, estudiar, hacer deporte, cenar, acostarse, dormir'. The main difficulties for English speakers are: 1) Reflexive verbs: Spanish uses reflexive pronouns (me, te, se, nos, os, se) that must agree with the subject. English speakers often forget these (e.g., 'yo levanto' instead of 'me levanto'). 2) The word order: in Spanish, the reflexive pronoun goes before the verb (me levanto), while in English, it's after the verb (I get up). 3) Some verbs have irregular conjugations in the present tense (e.g., almorzar, acostarse, dormir). 4) English speakers often confuse 'almorzar' (to have lunch) with 'comer' (to eat) or 'cenar' (to have dinner).
rule: Everyday verbs: levantarse (to get up), ducharse (to shower), lavarse (to wash), vestirse (to get dressed), desayunar (to have breakfast), ir al trabajo (to go to work), trabajar (to work), almorzar (to have lunch, o→ue), estudiar (to study), hacer deporte (to exercise), cenar (to have dinner), acostarse (to go to bed, o→ue), dormir (to sleep, o→ue). Reflexive verbs: the pronoun must agree with the subject: me (yo), te (tú), se (él/ella/usted), nos (nosotros), os (vosotros), se (ellos/ellas/ustedes).

common_error.explanation: The error is using the wrong reflexive pronoun ('se') for 'yo' and the wrong conjugation ('levanta'). In Spanish, the reflexive pronoun must match the subject: 'yo' uses 'me', not 'se'. Also, the verb must be conjugated correctly: 'levanto' for 'yo'. English speakers often confuse reflexive pronouns because they think of 'oneself' or use 'se' as a generic reflexive.

examples.original: Me levanto, me ducho y desayuno.
examples.translation: I get up, shower, and have breakfast.

examples.original: Me acuesto tarde los fines de semana.
examples.translation: I go to bed late on weekends.

title: Verbo gustar / The verb 'gustar' (to like)
bridge: In English, you say 'I like pizza' – where 'I' is the subject and 'pizza' is the object. In the target language, the verb 'gustar' works backwards: the thing you like becomes the subject, and the person who likes it becomes the indirect object. So 'I like pizza' is 'Me gusta la pizza' – literally 'Pizza pleases me'. This is very confusing for English speakers. Your most common mistake is using 'gustar' like a normal verb: 'Yo gusto la pizza' (incorrect). Another mistake is not matching the verb to the subject: 'Me gustan las pizzas' (plural subject) vs. 'Me gusta la pizza' (singular subject). Also, you often forget the indirect object pronoun (me, te, le, nos, os, les) and say 'Gusta la pizza' (missing 'me'). Remember: 'gustar' is only used in two forms in the present for A1: 'gusta' (singular subject) and 'gustan' (plural subject).
rule: Structure: indirect object pronoun + gusta/gustan + subject (the thing liked). Indirect object pronouns: me (to me), te (to you informal), le (to him/her/you formal), nos (to us), os (to you all informal Spain), les (to them/you all formal). Examples: 'Me gusta el chocolate' (I like chocolate – chocolate pleases me). 'Te gustan los libros' (You like books – books please you). 'A ella le gusta bailar' (She likes to dance – dancing pleases her). Use 'gusta' when the subject is singular or an infinitive. Use 'gustan' when the subject is plural. You can add 'a + person' at the beginning for emphasis or to clarify: 'A mí me gusta el café' (I like coffee).

common_error.explanation: You used 'gusto' as if it were a regular -ar verb (like 'hablo'). In the target language, 'gustar' works in reverse: the subject is the thing liked, not the person. The correct form is 'Me gusta la pizza' (pizza pleases me). This is one of the most frequent and persistent errors for English speakers.
common_error.ejemplo_2: A mí me gusta la pizza.

examples.original: A él le gusta el fútbol.

levels.label: Gustar + infinitivo
levels.translation: Gustar + infinitive (to like to do something)

title: Adjetivos descriptivos / Descriptive adjectives
bridge: In English, we say 'big, small, beautiful, ugly, young, old, new, happy, sad, intelligent, friendly, fun, easy, difficult, expensive, cheap, fast, slow'. In Spanish, it's similar: 'grande, pequeño, bonito, feo, joven, viejo, nuevo, alegre, triste, inteligente, simpático, divertido, fácil, difícil, caro, barato, rápido, lento'. The main difficulties for English speakers are: 1) Gender and number agreement: In Spanish, adjectives must agree in gender and number with the noun (e.g., 'casa bonita', 'coches bonitos').
